name: Atlanta
description: Atlanta, Georgia — Sherman's target, the 1996 Olympics, and the capital of the New South.

questions.q: Atlanta is the capital of which U.S. state?

source.notes: Became state capital in 1868.

questions.q: In 1996, Atlanta hosted which international sporting event?

source.notes: Centennial Olympic Games.

questions.q: Which iconic soft-drink company has its global headquarters in Atlanta?

source.notes: Coca-Cola has been Atlanta-based since 1886.

questions.q: Atlanta's Hartsfield-Jackson is the world's busiest airport by what measure?

source.notes: ATL has topped global passenger charts for most years since 1998.

questions.q: Which MLB team, moved to Atlanta from Milwaukee in 1966, plays its home games in the metro area?

source.notes: Braves won the World Series in 1995 and 2021.

questions.q: Which 24-hour news network was founded in Atlanta by Ted Turner in 1980?

source.notes: CNN Center was Atlanta's downtown landmark for decades.

questions.q: Atlanta is the birthplace of which civil-rights leader, whose historic home still stands in the city?

source.notes: MLK National Historical Park is on Auburn Avenue.

questions.q: Which Union general led the 1864 capture and burning of Atlanta during the Civil War?

source.notes: Precedes Sherman's March to the Sea.

questions.q: What was Atlanta's original railroad-terminus name in the 1830s?

source.notes: Later Marthasville (1843), then Atlanta (1847).

questions.q: What was Atlanta briefly renamed before gaining its current name?

source.notes: Named for Governor Lumpkin's daughter Martha.

questions.q: Which street serves as Atlanta's defining north–south spine, with dozens of variant names?

source.notes: At least 71 Atlanta streets contain 'Peachtree.'

questions.q: Which Atlanta park, a former Piedmont Exposition site, hosts the annual Peachtree Road Race finish?

source.notes: Olmsted Brothers–influenced design in Midtown.

questions.q: Atlanta's Peachtree Road Race is typically described as what?
choices: The largest marathon in the Americas

source.notes: Held every 4th of July.

questions.q: The December 15, 1939, world premiere of which sweeping Civil War epic took place in Atlanta?

source.notes: Loew's Grand Theatre, later destroyed by fire.

questions.q: Which historic 1929 theatre in Midtown Atlanta is a premier live-performance landmark today?

source.notes: Moorish Revival architecture, saved from demolition in the 1970s.

questions.q: Which Atlanta attraction was the largest aquarium in the world when it opened in 2005?

source.notes: Held the title until 2012.

questions.q: Atlanta's nickname, granted in recognition of its extensive tree canopy, is what?

source.notes: Atlanta also calls itself the 'Gate City of the South.'

questions.q: Atlanta sits primarily in which two Georgia counties?

source.notes: Small portion is in DeKalb; most of the city is in Fulton.

questions.q: Which 22-mile ring of former rail corridor is being redeveloped into a multi-use trail and park network in Atlanta?

source.original_a: BeltLine project and improvements to parks, sporting venues, and transportation infrastructure
source.notes: Concept originated in a 1999 Georgia Tech master's thesis.

questions.q: In 1917, a devastating fire leveled large portions of which Atlanta neighborhood?

source.notes: The May 1917 fire destroyed hundreds of homes in the Old Fourth Ward.

questions.q: Who was Atlanta's first African-American woman mayor, serving from 2002 to 2010?

source.notes: Maynard Jackson (1974) was Atlanta's first Black mayor.

questions.q: Which river forms part of Atlanta's northwestern boundary and supplies much of its drinking water?

source.notes: Atlanta draws most of its water from Lake Lanier, a Chattahoochee impoundment.
